coredata.dc:title: Nonlinear optical effects in one- and two-layer metal structures
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Khmelinskii, Igor
Makarov, Vladimir I.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we determined the spectral properties and frequency summing that induced superemission (SE) in two assemblies (A and B) built from a Co nanolayer and a Co nanotrack, which differed in terms of their topography. We found that homogeneous Co films with layer thicknesses of 7.101 and 11.755 nm produced discrete absorption spectra, which originated from the transverse quantum confinement operating in these metal films. The surface plasmon resonance in these films appeared at energies over 50000 cm−1 where a longer wavelength tail was readily observable. All of the nanolayers considered had discrete emission spectra and the emission bands strongly overlapped due to the large bandwidths. Their emission decay was described by a single-exponential function with lifetimes of 167 ± 5 and 231 ± 7 ns for assemblies A and B, respectively. Similarly, the absorption spectrum for the two-layer sandwich assembly comprised the superposition of the respective spectra for the individual Co nanolayers. We found that these assemblies could sum the pumping radiation photons to produce photons with higher energy. After exciting the 11.755 nm Co nanolayer to a given transition, the Co nanotrack produced emission at close to double the energy. We identified this emission as SE and the bandwidths were only about 32 ± 0.5 and 21 ± 3 cm−1 for assemblies A and B, respectively. The SE band appeared with the build-up time of 32 ± 2 ns for assembly A and 21± 1 ns for assembly B, and disappeared with decay times of 143 ± 5 and 113 ± 4 ns, respectively. We analyzed the energy transfer in detail based on the exchange mechanism, and the eventual contribution of the electric dipole–dipole mechanism was also estimated.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties of the sandwich assembly and structured nanolayer
Emission of the Co nanotrack at the summed energy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sample assemblies: (a) sample A comprising two layers: Co (11.755 nm), BN (10.1 nm), and a 10 μm Co (7.101 nm) track; (b) sample B comprising a Co layer (11.755 nm) with a thinner 10 μm wide Co track (7.101 nm).

IR and UV/Vis absorption spectra obtained for the isolated films and the sandwich structure deposited on CaF2: (a) 7.101 nm Co layer; (b) 11.755 nm Co layer; (c) Co (7.101 nm) – BN (11.1 nm) – Co (11.755 nm) assembly (sample A).
Emission spectra obtained for the individual thin films: (a) 7.101 nm Co film excited at 36849 cm−1; (b) 11.755 nm Co film excited at 18436 cm−1.
Emission signal decay: (1) Co – 7.101 nm; (2) Co – 11.755 nm. The oscilloscope was triggered 16 ns after the laser pulse to reduce the effects of scattered excitation light.

(a) Superemission spectra and (b) superemission kinetics recorded at the spectral emission maximum. Excitation was achieved by laser pulses at 18436 cm−1: (1) sample A (Fig. 1a); (2) sample B (Fig. 1b).
Superemission intensity as a function of the pumping pulse energy. The data were fitted by a second-order polynomial I SE = aE 2, where a = 0.36 mJ−2.

Superemission polarization showing the measured intensity as a function of the polarizer angle φ (circles). The data were fitted by
I
(
ϕ
)
= A C o
s
2
(
ϕ
)
. Angular distributions of the superemission intensity are shown in the vertical plane (squares) and in the plane of the assembly (triangles).

Schematic representations of the quantum states of the samples. Sample A (a): the initially prepared two-exciton state of the 11.755 nm Co layer and the electronic ground state of the 7.101 nm Co track; (b) Ground state of the layer and the excited state of the track. Sample B (c): the initially prepared two-exciton state of the 11.755 nm Co layer and the ground state of the 7.101 nm Co track; (d) Ground state of the layer and excited state of the track.
Average phonon energy in the Co model system as a function of the number of Co atoms in the layer using the: (a) first approach (dashed line); and (b) second approach (continuous line).

Dependence of the value of the energy transfer rate constant k
ET on the effective thickness a 0 of the BN nanolayer for sample A (Fig. 1a) at E = 36849 cm−1.
Absorption maxima in the IR and UV–Vis spectra for the Co films.
Emission band peaks in Co thin films. Bands were assigned using the data in Table 1.

row.entry: 7.101 nm film, ν exc = 36849 cm−1 n = 8 → n = 13
11.755 nm film, ν exc = 18436 cm−1 n = 9 → n = 15

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Values of the experimental parameters τ
j
−1 (j = 1, 2) and the fitting parameters k 1,relax , k
ET , and (k 2,relax + k
SE ). The subscripts i = 1, 2 in the fitting parameters refer to layer (1) or track (2) in each of the samples, respectively.

head.ce:title: Nonlinear optical effects in one- and two-layer metal structures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of the Algarve, FCT, DQF and CEOT, 8005-139, Faro, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Puerto Rico, Rio Piedras Campus, PO Box 23343, San Juan, PR, 00931-3343, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Physics, UPR, Rio Piedras Campus, PO Box 23343, San Juan, PR, 00931, USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we determined the spectral properties and frequency summing that induced superemission (SE) in two assemblies (A and B) built from a Co nanolayer and a Co nanotrack, which differed in terms of their topography. We found that homogeneous Co films with layer thicknesses of 7.101 and 11.755 nm produced discrete absorption spectra, which originated from the transverse quantum confinement operating in these metal films. The surface plasmon resonance in these films appeared at energies over 50000 cm−1 where a longer wavelength tail was readily observable. All of the nanolayers considered had discrete emission spectra and the emission bands strongly overlapped due to the large bandwidths. Their emission decay was described by a single-exponential function with lifetimes of 167 ± 5 and 231 ± 7 ns for assemblies A and B, respectively. Similarly, the absorption spectrum for the two-layer sandwich assembly comprised the superposition of the respective spectra for the individual Co nanolayers. We found that these assemblies could sum the pumping radiation photons to produce photons with higher energy. After exciting the 11.755 nm Co nanolayer to a given transition, the Co nanotrack produced emission at close to double the energy. We identified this emission as SE and the bandwidths were only about 32 ± 0.5 and 21 ± 3 cm−1 for assemblies A and B, respectively. The SE band appeared with the build-up time of 32 ± 2 ns for assembly A and 21± 1 ns for assembly B, and disappeared with decay times of 143 ± 5 and 113 ± 4 ns, respectively. We analyzed the energy transfer in detail based on the exchange mechanism, and the eventual contribution of the electric dipole–dipole mechanism was also estimated.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of functional nanostructures and nanomaterials is crucial for the production of new technologies in the areas of microelectronics and optoelectronics, including new photodetectors, sensors, and biosensors [1–3]. The unique optical properties of various nanostructured materials have been studied extensively during the last decades [4–8]. Quantum confinement (QC) is a very interesting phenomenon that is observed in nanostructured systems [9–11] when at least one of the dimensions is smaller than the electron de Broglie wavelength [12]. Typical examples of QC include three-dimensional (3D) quantum dots [13,14] and graphene quantum dots [15,16], two-dimensional (2D) nanotubes and nanofibers [17,18], and one-dimensional (1D) nanolayers [19–21]. In this study, we focused on 1D QC in metal and semiconductor nanofilms, and we also investigated optical absorption and emission by these films.

The optical properties of semiconductor nanofilms have received considerable attention [9–11,22–25]. In particular, in a recent study of InAs semiconductor nanolayers, the optical absorption properties of free-standing InAs nanomembranes with thicknesses of 3–19 nm were explored using Fourier transform IR spectroscopy [25], which showed that the absorption recorded at room temperature increased in a stepwise manner toward higher energies. These spectra were considered to have originated from the interband transitions between the subbands of the 2D InAs nanomembranes. The change in absorbance associated with each of the steps was ca. 1.6% and it was independent of the membrane thickness. These experimental results are consistent with the theoretically predicted absorbance quantum A
Q = πα/n
c , which is apparent as steps in the spectrum for each set of interband transitions in a 2D semiconductor, where α is the fine structure constant and n
c is an optical local field correction factor [25]. Given the unique optical properties of QC systems, it is possible to create population inversion between their electronic states, thereby leading to superemission (SE) and lasing. Semiconductor lasers have received much attention [26–30] and a good theoretical understanding has been achieved.

The optical properties of metal nanolayers have also been studied quite extensively [9–11,31–33]. Typically, all of these layers produce a plasmonic resonance band [31–33]. In addition, it was demonstrated that the absorption spectra produced by metal (Cr, Fe, Co, or Ni) nanolayers have multiple discrete bands [9–11], which can be described with very high accuracy based on the quantization of electronic states in a rectangular potential box with infinite walls, and the state energy is given by Ref. [34]:
(1)
E
n
=
π
2
h
2
n
2
2 m
a
2
,
where h is the Planck constant, n is the quantum number, m is the effective electron mass, and a is the box width. This model is apparently valid when the excited-state energy is several eV below the ionization limit of the respective metal. These quantized states in metal nanolayers offer the unique possibility of inducing SE and lasing, which are very well known in semiconductors and other systems. Indeed, we previously reported SE in Cr nanolayers [35], vertically aligned single-wall carbon nanotubes [36], a structured Co nanolayer [37], and a three-layer Co assembly [38]. Furthermore, this SE is facilitated by the QC observed in homogeneous nanolayers and nanotubes [39–42]. It should be noted that in all of these cases [35,37,38] and other previously reported examples [9–11], 1D QC operates in the direction perpendicular to the layer's surface. The effective electron mass in Eq. (1) is an empirical parameter that depends on the material and it is smaller than the free electron mass [34]. As mentioned above, we previously reported SE in Cr and Co nanolayers [35,37] as well as in a three-layer Co assembly [38], which we attributed to the properties of the electronic states produced by QC. We found that these nanolayers produced discrete absorption spectra in the mid-infrared (IR), visible (Vis), and ultraviolet (UV) range. We observed SE in planar Cr and Co nanolayers after laser excitation into one of the higher-energy transitions, where the population inversion was created with respect to the lower-lying unpopulated excited states. A detailed analysis of the absorption spectra for the Cr and Co nanolayers determined effective electron masses of 0.047 and 0.171 of the free electron mass, respectively. Nonlinear SE explained by summing the photon energies was also observed in a three-layer Co assembly [38]. This assembly was excited by two laser pulses in the Vis range, and SE from the middle Co nanolayer was observed in the UV where it was directed along the surface of the middle layer. These studies directly demonstrated the existence of 1D QC in metal nanolayers.

In this study, we determined nonlinear effects in an assembly with a two-layer sandwich structure (sample A) and in a second assembly with a single structured layer (sample B). The two Co patterns in sample A were separated by a BN insulator layer. We recorded the absorption spectra for the separately prepared Co layers and for the sandwich assembly, where a discrete level structure clearly appeared in all of the spectra. The spectra were recorded separately in the IR (500–8500 cm−1) and UV–Vis spectral ranges (12000–52000 cm−1). We also recorded SE at 30883 cm−1 generated by the thinner Co track (7.101 ± 0.012 nm), while the thicker Co layer (11.755 ± 0.013 nm) was pumped at 18436 cm−1. This SE was attributable to the simultaneous energy transfer of two photons from the excited states of the thicker Co layer (11.755 nm) to the excited state of the thinner Co track (7.101 nm) at 36849 cm−1, and SE occurred to the first excited state of the thinner Co track (7.101 nm) located at 5966 cm−1. This frequency summing is a nonlinear phenomenon that occurs in a metal sandwich structure at very moderate laser intensities due to the relatively long lifetimes (ca. 100 ns) of the excited states involved.

CaF2 substrates with a surface area of 25 × 12.5 mm2 and thickness of 1 mm (Esco Optics) were used to deposit the samples. Commercial Co targets and ammonia borane (Sigma/Aldrich) were used to deposit the nanocrystalline films with a commercial sputtering/thermo-evaporation Benchtop Turbo deposition system (Denton Vacuum) and GSL-1100X-50-LVT vacuum tube furnace (MTI Corporation), respectively. The Co nanolayers were deposited by sputtering with the substrate at 475 °C in all of the experiments. The deposited films were annealed for 2 h at 900 °C and atmospheric pressure under pure nitrogen gas. Homogeneous BN nanolayers were deposited by chemical vapor deposition in a vacuum tube furnace using a previously reported synthesis method [43]. The substrate with the previously deposited Co nanolayer was kept at 1000 °C during synthesis and ammonia boron vapor flowed through the reaction zone in nitrogen as the carrier gas. The ammonia boron vapor was generated by heating the powder to 55–120 °C. The film thickness was controlled by X-ray diffraction [44] with an XPert MRD system (PANalytic), which was calibrated using standard nanofilms made from the same materials. The estimated absolute uncertainty of the film thickness was 0.2% but the relative uncertainties were much smaller, and they were determined by the shutter opening times in the deposition system. The structures of the sample assemblies are shown in Fig. 1 .

Absorption and emission spectra were recorded with a Hitachi U-3900H UV–Vis Spectrophotometer and Edinburgh Instruments FS5 Spectrofluorometer, respectively. The absorption spectra in the middle- and near-IR were recorded using a PF2000 FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer). The absorption spectra were represented as the difference in the transmission and reflection spectra. The spectral peak maxima were located precisely using PeakFit software (Sigmaplot). The polynomials were fitted and the fitting uncertainties were estimated using the LINEST function in Excel (Microsoft). Photo-induced response measurements were obtained using a high-pressure Xe lamp (1000W, Ariel Corporation, Model 66023), monochromator (Thermo Jarrell Ash, Mono Spec/50), DET10A biased Si detector (THORLABS, supplied with the spectral calibration curve), model 2182A nanovoltmeter (Keithley Instruments) connected to a computer by a General Purpose Interface Bus (GPIB) interface, and self-designed software in the LabView programming environment (National Instruments). The light beam of the Xe lamp was filtered by interference filters with the bandpass at 542 nm, and radiation excited the surface of the 11.755 nm layer. We detected the emissions from the nanotrack in both samples along the track axis, as shown in Fig. 1.

We recorded the time-resolved emission using the fundamental harmonics of a dye laser for excitation, i.e., LPD-2000 (Λ-Physics) with coumarin-4 dye (with frequency multiplication on a BBO crystal; Λ-Physics). The fundamental frequency of coumarin-4 dye could be tuned in the spectral range of 370–580 nm (frequency-doubled to obtain radiation at 230–290 nm), and pumped by the fourth harmonics of a YAG laser (266 nm, Surelight-II, Continuum Inc.). The laser pulse duration was about 7–10 ns. The dye laser radiation was defocused onto the entire free surface of the relevant layer. The emission was collected by a 30-cm spherical CaF2 lens and detected by a photodiode (PD; DET10A biased Si detector from THORLABS) or a photomultiplier (Hamamatsu, Hamamatsu-PMT-H9305-03) after passing through a neutral density filter. The data acquisition system comprised a PC, digital oscilloscope (WaveSurfer 400 series, LeCroy), two digital delay generators (DG-535, Stanford Research), two boxcar integrators (SR-250, Stanford Research), fast amplifier (SR-240, Stanford Research), and a computer interface board (SR-245 Stanford Research). For the spectroscopy experiments, the fluorescence signal was monitored with the digital oscilloscope and averaged, typically using five laser pulses per frequency step. The output energy was controlled, and the monochromator scan operated using a PD and PCI-6034E DAQ I/O board (National Instruments), where the control code in the LABVIEW environment ran on a second PC. These experimental methods obtained a time resolution of 2.5 ns.

We measured the emission quantum yields using a calibrated radiation source (High Pressure Hg lamp: ESI 1200 100W MSR Lamp; Planet Bulb Inc.) and a calibrated photodiode (DET10A Si Detector from THORLABS).
All of the measurements were performed in an optical cryostat (Optistat DN-V2, Oxford Instruments) at 77 K unless indicated otherwise.

We determined the steady-state absorption and emission, time resolved emission, and emission at the summed frequency. First, we present the absorption and emission spectra, and the emission kinetics of the separate Co layers, where each was deposited on a CaF2 substrate. We then describe the properties of the sandwich assemblies shown in Fig. 1.

The absorption spectra were obtained by subtracting the transmittance and reflectance from the normalized 100% transmission spectrum. Fig. 2 shows the mid-IR and UV/Vis absorption spectra determined for the individual Co films and the assembled Co (7.101 nm)-BN (10.1 nm)-Co (11.755 nm) sandwich structure (sample A).

The absorption spectra shown in Fig. 2 were obtained by considering the reflection spectra of the thin films. It should be noted that the absorption spectra in Fig. 2 contained discrete spectral bands in both the mid-IR and UV/Vis spectral ranges. The spectral maxima were determined using PeakFit software and the results are listed in Table 1 .

The results in Table 1 may be simulated with perfect accuracy using a previously described relationship [34]:
(2)
Δ
E
n , n + m
=
E
n + m
−
E
n
= 3029.2
1
f
a
2
[
2 m n +
m
2
]
,
where n is the quantum number of the highest occupied electronic state, n + m is the quantum number of the excited electronic state (considering single-electron transitions only), f = m
eff
/m
e is the ratio of the effective electron mass relative to the free electron mass, and a is the nanolayer thickness in nm. By applying Eq. (2) to the data in Table 1, we found that the band at 11266 cm−1 was not recorded experimentally in the third column for the 11.755 nm Co layer. Simulating the data in Table 1 produced a value of f = 0.171 in both films and n = 8 and 9 for the 7.101 and 11.755 nm films, respectively. These values are in good agreement with the data obtained in our previous studies [37,38]. In the following, we explore the emission spectra obtained from the Co nanolayers by exciting specific electronic transitions.

The steady-state emission spectra were recorded at different excitation energies (36848 cm−1 for the 7.101 nm film and 18436 cm−1 for the 11.755 nm films) with the film/substrate at 45° relative to both the excitation and emission windows, and the excitation beam reflected off the film and away from the emission detector. The emission spectra are shown in Fig. 3 , where the scattered excitation light was removed by subtracting the respective deconvoluted band.

The recorded spectra were analyzed using the PeakFit package. Three bands were identified in the first spectrum and two in the second, and the band maxima are listed in Table 2 . It should be noted that the emission spectra also had a discrete structure, although the emission bands overlapped considerably due to their relatively large bandwidths (1300–2100 cm−1).

The emission transitions were assigned using the data in Table 1 and Eq. (1). It should be noted that the emission maxima were red-shifted in all of the samples compared with the respective absorption peaks, which we interpreted as a consequence of excited state relaxation as well as the transition intensity determined by the phonon-exciton overlap integral and the thermal distribution of the phonons in the electronic ground state [38]. We measured the emission quantum yields where values of 0.59 and 0.63 were obtained for the assemblies in Fig. 1a and b, respectively.

Time-resolved experiments were conducted for the 7.101 nm and 11.755 nm samples. The n = 8 → n = 13 and n = 9 → n = 15 transitions were excited at 36849 cm−1 (Co = 7.101 nm) and 18436 cm−1 (Co = 11.755 nm) using an appropriate pulsed laser. Emissions were detected around ν
emiss = 30803 and 15978 cm−1, respectively, for the transitions listed in Table 2 and selected by the monochromator. Fig. 4 shows the recorded emission decay curves.

Each decay curve was fitted to an exponential function with lifetimes of 167 ± 5 and 231 ± 7 ns for curves 1 and 2, respectively. Next, we investigated the spectral properties of sample A in Fig. 1a, which we compared with those of the individual thin films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the sandwich assembly and structured nanolayer

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2c shows the absorption spectrum recorded for the three-layer system comprising the sample A assembly built of two Co layers (7.101 and 11.755 nm) separated by a BN nanolyer (10.11 nm). It should be noted that the absorption spectrum of the sandwich assembly indicated the spectral superposition of the two individual layers, although it was somewhat different from the sum of the optical spectra for the individual nanolayers. These spectra should have been similar to those measured in the nanotrack zone for sample A, as shown in Fig. 1a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission of the Co nanotrack at the summed energy
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, pulsed laser experiments were conducted by exciting the Co nanolayer (as shown in Fig. 1a and b) using a laser pulse at 18436 cm−1 (excitation of the n = 9 → n = 15 transition in the 11.755 nm Co layer). The emission induced in the Co track (7.101 nm; Fig. 1a and b) was detected along its axis, and the recorded emission spectra are shown in Fig. 5 . Similar to our previous studies, we interpreted this narrow-band emission as SE.

Fig. 5a shows that the emission band maximum was at 30882 ± 1 cm−1 and the widths were 32 ± 0.5 cm−1 for sample A and 21 ± 0.3 cm−1 for sample B. We then characterized the mechanism that we considered responsible for producing these emissions.

The excitation energy of 18436 cm−1 corresponded to the n = 9 → n = 15 transition in the 11.755 nm Co layer. The n = 13 state in the 7.101 nm Co layer was populated by the two-photon energy transfer from the 11.755 nm layer, which was induced by interlayer exchange interactions. The two-photon transition occurred in quasiresonance. The SE from the n = 15 excited state of the thinner Co track occurred as the n = 9 ←n = 15 transition at 2ν
pump
– ν 8,9(7.101 nm) = 30883 cm−1, where ν
pump = 18436 cm−1 and ν 8,9(7.101 nm) = 5966 cm−1. The n = 9 state of Co (7.101 nm) was the first excited state, so it was not populated at the measurement temperature, and the population inversion was created between the n = 15 and n = 9 states, where the observed emission was identified as SE. Thus, the theoretic SE energy very closely reproduced the experimental emission spectrum in Fig. 5a.

Fig. 5b shows the SE decay kinetics. We fitted the decays in Fig. 5b with a biexponential function:
(3)
I
S E
(
t
)
=
A
0
e
−
(
t −
τ
0
δ τ
)
2
+
A
1
(
e
−
t
τ
1
−
e
−
t
τ
2
)
,
with four fitting parameters comprising A 0, A 1, τ 1, and τ 2, and using τ 0 = δτ/2 ≈ 4 ns. However, we were only interested in the lifetimes, with τ 1 = 32 ± 2 ns and τ 2 = 143 ± 5 ns (sample A, Fig. 1a), and τ 1 = 21 ± 1 ns and τ 2 = 113 ± 4 ns (sample B, Fig. 1b). It should be noted that in previous studies, we obtained significantly shorter kinetic parameters for the SE of a Co assembly with a different configuration [37,38]. The differences can be attributed to the specific configurations of the assemblies [37,38]. We then investigated the time-resolved measurements in details.
We also recorded the dependence of the emission energy on the excitation energy and the results are shown in Fig. 6 .
As expected, the experimental data were fitted quite well by a second-order polynomial, thereby providing additional evidence for the SE generated in a two-photon process.

We explored the SE polarization by recording its intensity at the spectral maximum as a function of the polarizer rotation angle by using the experimental data shown in Fig. 7 , as well as fitting with Cos2(φ), where φ is the polarizer rotation angle.

The SE intensity peaked at zero angle, where the electric field E vector was perpendicular to the sample surface, which was expected based on the transition selection rules, and it was normal to the nanotrack axis. It should be noted that in our assemblies, we again observed long-distance excitation energy transfer because most of the energy supplied to the entire nanofilm eventually arrived at the nanotrack by traveling over macroscopic distances [37,38].

We recorded the angular distribution of the SE intensity by moving the photodetector around the source in the horizontal plane of the assembly, as well as in the vertical plane perpendicular to the assembly surface along a circumference with a radius of 30 cm. The plots were fitted with Gaussian functions with a half-width of 33.9° in the horizontal plane and 11.9° in the vertical plane. Thus, the emission was strongly anisotropic, which is usual for SE.

The SE quantum yields were measured for samples A and B. The excitation energy absorbed by the 11.755 nm Co layer was calculated by considering its absorption spectrum (Fig. 2b), where we integrated the optical density over the laser emission band and the average laser pulse energy was measured independently. The SE pulse energy was measured using a calibrated photomultiplier tube (see Section 2). The SE quantum yields were estimated at 0.11 for sample A (Figs. 1a) and 0.14 for sample B (Fig. 1b). These quantum yields were higher than the value of 0.063 obtained previously for a multilayer device [38].

We assumed that the quasiresonant two-exciton energy transfer occurring via the exchange mechanism from the excited 11.755 nm Co layer to the ground-state 7.101 nm Co track (Fig. 1a and 1b) led to the SE of the track. The operator of the exchange interaction may be represented as follows [37,38]:
(4)

e , 2
|
where the subscript 1 refers to the 11.755 nm Co layer and the subscript 2 to the 7.101 nm Co track, as shown in Fig. 1, while r
e,i is the location of the optical electron in the respective Co nanostructure and e is the electron charge. To analyze the probability of the two-exciton energy transfer, we defined the potential surfaces of the assemblies as shown in Fig. 8 , where the z axis was perpendicular to the Co layer surface and it passed through its center point.

We analyzed the energy transfer problem using the 1D potential surface with infinite potential walls. The electronic states of the layer and the track were not separated by a potential barrier (Fig. 1a). The initial state of the system is given by:

2
.

The average frequency
ω ¯
was calculated in Co nanocrystals using ab initio methods, where the lattice parameter values and cluster structure were based on the previously reported data [49] listed in Table 3 .

Two independent ab initio approaches were employed. In the first approach, the commercial Gaussian-2000 software package was employed to calculate the fundamental frequencies of a Co nanocrystal using the coupled clusters method with the 6-31G(d) basis set. The total number of atoms in the 3D model of the layer structure varied from 128 to 324. The layer thickness was equal to the lattice parameter C (see Table 3). These calculations were performed in the ROCK CLUSTER environment. The output data from these ab initio calculations were used as the input data for our self-designed FORTRAN code, which calculated the average phonon frequency for our system. The first approach produced the average frequency values as a function of the number of Co atoms, as shown in Fig. 9 .

The second theoretical approach obtained better results. This previously proposed approach [50] employed the first-principles pseudopotential method within the spin density functional theory. Ultrasoft atomic pseudopotentials [51] and the generalized gradient approximation [52] were used for the exchange and correlation potentials within the Quantum-ESPRESSO package [53]. We analyzed the same Co nanolayer structure, where a Γ-centered 6 × 6 × 13 k mesh was used to sample the irreducible Brillouin zone in the Monkhorst–Pack scheme [54]. To improve the accuracy of the analysis, the Methfessel–Paxton smearing technique was used with a smearing width of 0.001 Ry [55] and convergence better than 5 × 10−6 eV was achieved for the total energy. We used the previously reported crystal-lattice parameters listed in Table 3 [49]. The values calculated for the phonon frequencies as a function of the number of Co atoms in the model system were used in our self-designed FORTRAN code, which calculated the average frequency values. The values calculated for the average phonon frequency as a function of the Co atom number are also plotted in Fig. 9.

Fig. 9 shows that both ab initio approaches produced similarly decreasing average phonon frequencies as the number of Co atoms increased, with a common asymptotic value of 93 cm−1, which we used in the current estimates.

The numerical calculations were conducted with a self-designed FORTRAN code. The calculation for sample A assumed that a 0 × U 0 = 8 × 10−2 eV × nm [38], where a 0 is the effective thickness of the BN nanolayer. We used this parameter and E = 36849 cm−1 to calculate k
ET as a function of a 0 for sample A and the results are shown in Fig. 10 .

The results in Fig. 10 correspond to an energy transfer rate constant value of 7.41 × 107 Hz for the barrier thickness a 0 = 2.5 Å, or 3.49 × 107 Hz for a 0 = 100 Å. The same parameter estimated for the Co sandwich assembly with the metal layers separated by BN layers at a thickness of about 10 nm was larger by at least one order of magnitude [38]. We consider that this difference could be explained by the different structures of the two assemblies. The same k
ET value calculated for sample B (Fig. 1b) with the state diagram shown in Fig. 8b equaled 5.72 × 107 Hz. These values are quite reasonable and the proposed mechanism describes the experimental results adequately. Next, we investigated the SE kinetics.

The transient decay kinetics of the SE were fitted by function (2). Several processes occurred simultaneously to cause the SE, including laser excitation of the film (subscript 1 in the reaction scheme), and energy transfer to the track (subscript 2). These processes can be represented by the following reaction scheme:

where W 0 is the laser pulse amplitude, τ is the laser pulse duration, D 1 is the absorption optical density of the Co1 layer at a specific excitation wavelength, k
i,relax are the energy relaxation rate constants, k
ET is the rate constant for the energy transfer, and τ
SE is the SE lifetime. The master equations for the proposed mechanism may be represented as follows:

Using Eq. (18), we fitted the data in Fig. 6. The parameters k 1,relax , k
ET , and (k 2,relax + k
SE ) were estimated based on the fittings listed in Table 4 .

According to the data in Table 4, we conclude that the build-up rate constant τ 1 −1 in column 2 apparently coincides with k 2,relax + k
SE in column 6, and the decay rate constant τ 2 −1 in column 3 coincides with k 1,relax in column 4.

In previous studies, we reported the discrete structure of the absorption spectra for various metal and semiconductor films [9–11,35–38]. This structure is apparently caused by the 1D QC operating in the direction normal to the film surface. It should be noted that the well-known surface plasmon theory [50–54] only predicts one plasmon resonance in these films and it cannot explain the experimentally observed multiple peaks. We assume that in the films investigated in the present study, the surface plasmon resonance band was located at energies above 50000 cm−1 and the tail appeared at the UV end of the spectral range explored. We also consider that this band was much stronger and wider than the lower-energy bands generated by QC. These results are in agreement with previous findings [55,56].

The properties of gold, silver, palladium, and platinum layers were studied by Slepicka et al. [55], where the metal layers were sputtered on polyethylene terephthalate films. However, the optical properties of these films were not explored. Ranjgar et al. [56] studied Ag(Au)/SiO2 nanostructured thin films fabricated on n-type silicon substrates via radio-frequency magnetron sputtering, where they analyzed the crystal structure, surface topography, and optical properties of these films using X-ray diffractometry, atomic force microscopy, and UV–Vis spectrophotometry, respectively. The optical absorption spectrum of the Ag/SiO2 films contained one surface plasmon resonance peak at 310 nm, but the same peak in Au/SiO2 was red-shifted to ca. 450 nm. However, these films were too thick for the QC bands to be apparent and the emission spectra were not probed [56]. Previously, we also reported detailed data regarding the emission spectra of metal nanolayers [9–11,35–38].

As mentioned above, we interpreted the emission at 30883 cm−1 as the SE generated by the n = 9 ← n = 15 transition in the Co 7.101 nm nanotrack. This emission had the usual properties of a SE in terms of the pumping energy dependence, angular distribution, and polarization [57,58]. The SE was observed along the track axis at an energy of 2ν
pump
– ν 8,9(7.101 nm), where ν
pump = 18436 cm−1, and ν 8,9(7.101 nm) = 5966 cm−1. The SE spectra obtained for both assemblies (Fig. 5a) were much narrower than the usual emission spectra (Fig. 3). Therefore, we positively identify SE in both assemblies. However, the total SE quantum yields calculated by assuming that the nanotrack emitted equally in both directions were quite low for the two assemblies, where they were equal to 0.11 for assembly A (Figs. 1a) and 0.14 for assembly B (Fig. 1b), because we simultaneously observed regular emissions in the direction perpendicular to the Co layer, which reduced the SE quantum yield.

By contrast, the emission spectra obtained for the isolated Co layers exhibited a regular distribution of the emission intensity over different bands (see Fig. 3). The emission quantum yields equaled 0.59 and 0.63 for assemblies A and B, respectively, and SE was not observed. It should be noted that we observed the SE in a previous study with a different geometry [3] and we also discussed the mechanism of state population inversion [35–38]. Thus, a Co track was combined with a Co layer that was thinner than the track, where the latter functioned as the energy trap and received most of the excitation energy provided to the nanolayer. SE was also generated along the axis of the track. These results were also interpreted using a mechanism based on the energy transfer induced by the exchange interaction between the states in the layer and the track [37,38].

In this and our previous studies, we investigated the energy transfer between nanostructures in detail using the exchange mechanism [37,38]. Next, we evaluated the possible role of the electric dipole–dipole mechanism in these systems. The dipole–dipole interaction is described by the Förster relationship [59]:
(19)

where the subscripts 1 and 2 are the numbers of electrons in the layer (see Fig. 1), the subscripts D and A correspond to the energy donor (layer) and energy acceptor (track) structural elements, respectively, r is the distance between the centers of the respective oscillators, and r
D and r
A are the distances between the centers of the oscillators and the optical electrons in the donor and acceptor. For the following analysis, Eq. (7) may be rewritten as:

2
.

We calculated the two-electron energy transfer rate constant based on the electric dipole–dipole mechanism using Eq. (8) and 93 cm−1 as the averaged vibrational frequency in the Co track. The FORTRAN code modified for this analysis was used to calculate the rate constants of interest for samples A and B (Fig. 1a and b), where the values obtained were 1.3 × 105 s−1 and 7.4 × 106 s−1, respectively. Therefore, the dipole–dipole mechanism made a negligible contribution for sample A, but a more noticeable one for sample B where it explained 14% of the energy transfer rate constant.

In this study, we investigated the absorption and emission spectra of Co nanolayers with different thicknesses. QC was observed in both the absorption and emission spectra obtained for the individual Co thin films. A surface plasmon resonance band was indicated at energies exceeding 50000 cm−1 because its tail appeared in the absorption spectra, as shown in Fig. 2. This resonance band should shift to lower energies in thicker layers. The emission bands were assigned to optical transitions from the excited electronic states to lower-lying excited and ground states. Samples A and B exhibited exciton summing, thereby leading to SE at the summed energy generated along the axis of the central Co track. The SE spectral bands were much narrower than those in the regular emission spectra. These results were explained by the exchange-mechanism energy transfer from the excited 11.755 nm Co nanolayer to the 7.101 nm Co nanotrack, which generated the SE (see Fig. 1). We also estimated the role of the electric dipole–dipole mechanism in the energy transfer and it was more significant for sample B (Fig. 1b).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from a PR NASA EPSCoR grant (NASA Cooperative Agreement NNX15AK43A) to V.M.
